1.body: Download Links Download v1.0.2 Download v1.0.1 Download v0.2.1 Download v0.2.0
1.title: Downloads

11.body: The organization id for the current project "orgName": "dev.interfiber.pak"
11.title: orgName [String]

12.body:
Array of components to build into the final package "components": [ "default"
]
12.title: components [Array]

13.body: Components are declared as objects in the project file like this: "component_super_epic": { } the name of the object must start with component then a underscore and a name with no spaces To compile the component into the package the name must be listed in the components array.
13.title: Section: Components

14.body: Name of the component displayed to the user, this can contain spaces.
14.title: $name [String]

15.body: Name of the component WITHOUT SPACES , used to generate the raw packages
15.title: $pkgName [String]

17.body: Name of the folder containing the payload located in the payloads folder.
17.title: $payloadName [String]

18.body: If the component should be selectable when the user is choosing what packages to be installed
18.title: $selectable [Bool]

19.body: If the component should start selected when the user is choosing what packages to be installed
19.title: $selected [Bool]

2.body: Pak builds MacOS package installers from a project file. Since apple removed PackageBuilder from xcode it became harder to build packages for software on MacOS, this is what pak aims to fix.
2.title: What does pak do?

20.body: If the component should be visible to the user when choosing what packages to be installed
20.title: $visible [Bool]

21.body: Relative path to the folder containing scripts for the package For more info on scripts see the Scripts section
21.title: $scriptsFolder [Path]

22.body:
Appearance settings apply to the entire installer, the Appearance config allows you to change background image, license, readme, welcome, and more. "appearance": { "comment": "Appearance config goes inside this json object"
}
22.title: Section: Appearance

23.body: Relative path to the text file used for the software license. During the install the user will need to agree to this license in order to continue
23.title: $license [Path]

24.body: Relative path to the html file used for the welcome screen. The rendered html will be displayed to the user when the installer launches
24.title: $welcomeHtml [Path]

25.body: Relative path to the html file used for the conclusion screen. The rendered html will be displayed to the user when the install finishes with success
25.title: $conclusionHtml [Path]

26.body: Relative path to the text file used for the readme screen. The contents of the readme file will be showed to the user before the install starts
26.title: $readme [Path]

27.body: Tells pak if it should look for a background config, default is false
27.title: $enableBackground [Bool]

28.body:
The background config is a subsection of the Appearance section, as the config for it will be placed inside the Appearance object likle below: "appearance": { "$backgroundConfig": {}
} Note that pak will not look for a background config unless $enableBackground is set to true in the Appearance config
28.breadcrumbs: Project File Docs » Section: Background config
28.title: Section: Background config

29.body: Relative path to the image file used as the background
29.title: $imageFile [Path]

30.body: Align type for the image, types are: center, left, right, top, bottom, topleft, topright, bottomleft, and bottomright
30.title: $align [String]

31.body: Installer scripts are files that contain code to be execute during the install process
31.title: Installer Scripts

32.body: To declare a folder to load scripts from we need to set $scriptsFolder in the component we want to add scripts to like below: "$scriptsFolder": "scripts" This will tell pak to build the component using any scripts from the scripts folder.
32.breadcrumbs: Installer Scripts » Adding scripts to a component
32.title: Adding scripts to a component

33.body: To add a preinstall script, create a new file in the folder storing you're scripts called preinstall this should have no file extension Then make the file executable like this: chmod +x preinstall The code inside preinstall by default uses shell script, but it can be changed by adding a shebang
33.title: Preinstall scripts

34.body: To add a postinstall script, create a new file in the folder storing you're scripts called postinstall this should have no file extension Then make the file executable like this: chmod +x postinstall The code inside postinstall by default uses shell script, but it can be changed by adding a shebang
34.title: Postinstall scripts

35.body: Pakr is the GUI client for pak
35.title: Pakr

38.body: By using the main pak installer you can enter the customize screen, and select pakr.
38.title: Manually

4.body: You can install pak via cargo using the following command: cargo install pakcli
4.title: With Cargo

40.body: The installers are NOT codesigned, which means macOS will not open it. To open it RIGHT CLICK the file in finder and click open.
40.breadcrumbs: Pakr » Pakr Install » Notice about codesign
40.title: Notice about codesign

42.body: Once pakr is installed, open a terminal and run the following command: pakr This will launch a instance of pakr
42.title: Launch pakr

43.body: In the pakr welcome interface enter the path to the project you wish to create, the destination folder SHOULD NOT exist.
43.breadcrumbs: Pakr » Pakr Functions » Create a project
43.title: Create a project

44.body: To load a pakr project launch the pakr program with one argument to the location of the project like below pakr $HOME/my_example_project Then in the interface select the Load Project Info button.
44.breadcrumbs: Pakr » Pakr Functions » Load a project
44.title: Load a project

45.body: To create a component make sure the project is loaded, then enter the component name and click Add Component like below. Pakr Example 2
45.breadcrumbs: Pakr » Pakr Functions » Create a component
45.title: Create a component

46.body: Once all changes to a component are made, select the Save/Build Project button to save the project.
46.breadcrumbs: Pakr » Pakr Functions » Save a component
46.title: Save a component

47.body: To edit a component make sure the project is loaded, then enter the component name and click ```Edit Component``. After changes are made make sure the save the component.
47.breadcrumbs: Pakr » Pakr Functions » Edit a component
47.title: Edit a component

5.body: If you want to install pak manually you can go to the main page, and download the latest version. This will download a pkg installer which can then be ran to install pak.
5.title: Manually

6.body:
If you use homebrew you can install pak using the following commands: brew tap Interfiber/homebrew-tap
brew install --cask pak
6.title: With Homebrew

7.body: The installers are NOT codesigned, which means macOS will not open it. To open it RIGHT CLICK the file in finder and click open.
7.title: Notice about codesign

8.body: Docs for the pak.project.json file in every pak project
8.breadcrumbs: Project File Docs » Project File Docs
8.title: Project File Docs

9.body: The name of the project, also displayed as the title in the installer "projectName": "Epic Project"
9.title: projectName [String]
